items.q: What is the 'race to the bottom' critique of globalisation?
items.correct: The argument that TNCs play countries off against each other, relocating to those with lowest wages, weakest regulation and lowest taxes — pressuring governments to cut standards to attract investment.
items.misconception: The race to the bottom means TNCs compete with each other for the lowest prices.
items.random_wrong: Race to the bottom is when companies offshore work to mines and underground factories.
items.why: It's about regulatory competition between states, not price competition between firms. It explains why LICs may keep labour and environmental rules low — competing for FDI rather than improving workers' conditions.

items.q: What happens during an El Nino event?
items.correct: Trade winds weaken, warm water that normally pools in the western Pacific spreads east, disrupting weather patterns globally — drought in Australia, floods in South America.
items.misconception: El Nino is when the ocean gets colder near Peru.
items.random_wrong: El Nino is the name of a specific hurricane that hit Mexico.
items.why: El Nino brings WARM water to Peru — the cold-water state is La Nina. Students mix up the two phases; the name 'the Christ child' refers to warm currents at Christmas.

items.q: What is the difference between weather and climate?
items.correct: Weather is short-term atmospheric conditions at a specific time and place; climate is the long-term (typically 30-year) average of weather patterns in a region.
items.misconception: Weather happens outside and climate happens globally.
items.random_wrong: Weather refers to rain and climate refers to temperature.
items.why: Students treat them as different phenomena rather than different timescales. The key distinction is time — climate is statistical, weather is instantaneous.
Correct answer: Anopheles. “Aedes” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which day was COVID-19 declared a pandemic by the WHO?
items.why: Correct answer: 11 March 2020. “1 January 2020” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Approximate annual TB deaths globally (in millions)?
items.why: Exact answer: 1.3. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
